title: Amending the Rules of the House of Representatives to permit certain resolutions to be privileged only if they are based on conduct which was the subject of an investigation and report by the appropriate committee of jurisdiction or if they are offered by direction of a party caucus or conference.
plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for bringing serious resolutions to the House floor for quick votes. These resolutions include impeaching government officials, punishing House members through censure or expulsion, or removing the Speaker or committee leaders. Under the new rules, these resolutions can only get special priority if a committee has already investigated the issue and written a report about it, or if a political party group officially directs that the resolution be brought forward. This makes it harder for individual members to bring these important resolutions to a vote without proper investigation or party support.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.
subjects: Government ethics and transparency, public corruption

sections.title: Section 1  Limitation on Privileged Status of Certain Resolutions
sections.text: This section changes Rule Nine of the House of Representatives rules by adding new requirements. Under the new rule, certain types of resolutions can only get special priority treatment (called privileged status) under two conditions. First, if the committee responsible for the issue has done a full investigation and written a report recommending that the House should take the action described in the resolution. Second, if a political party group (called a caucus or conference) officially directs that the resolution be introduced. The types of resolutions that must meet these requirements are those that impeach a government officer (remove them from office for wrongdoing) or those that punish House members through censure (formal criticism), reprimand (official scolding), or expulsion (removal from Congress). These requirements also apply to resolutions that would force the Speaker of the House or committee leaders to give up their leadership positions.
